text: This meeting is being recorded.
Hi, Carmen.
Hey, Andi.
How are you?
Good.
How are you?
I cannot complain.
It has been one of those days in CSM life where you're just like, what's next?
But in the best way, I got on a call with a customer.
going to say.
It wasn't a real, I'm ex-sales, so you will see me.
I get very excited about growth.
It's unnatural.
It's unhealthy for a CSM.
But I can't help it because if you spend more money, you get more features, you're sticky, you don't want to leave.
Retention is organic.
I love organic retention.
Retention.
But I had a customer earlier today that they were just pretty much telling us, you know, that, yeah, they love the tool, but they're looking over here.
And I immediately hear...
Another product that we acquired earlier this year.
And I just started getting all excited because I already know that our next call will be just a demo proof of concept.
This works.
This is your solution.
So I can't help but be giddy.
And then I got our email back or a Slack back that for 2000 licenses, you're looking at 120k upsell.
And honestly, just made my whole day.
Yeah, mean, fingers crossed.
Oh, man.
I love it.
I love growth.
I'd love to see it.
But hi, very random.
I'm sorry.
It's the middle of the work week.
And it's just, it's just a good day.
So I feel like it's very rare that I get on the phone with people and they're not like everything's on fire.
I don't know what's happening.
Not like internally, but like, you know, just in general in the world, like for life, there's not a lot of chaos is better than coffee.
Yeah.
So happy to hear somebody that's having a good day.
Cool.
So
I'm Andi.
Nice to meet you.
And just to give you a little bit of a sense of kind of who we are, where we are in our growth, and what this role looks like, I'll start by doing a quick intro and background.
We can hop into your experience, and I will leave at least 10 minutes for you to ask questions at the end.
And so just, I, I'm lead customer operations at GrowthX.
I have been here for two and half months now, which feels like a long time at a company that's less than a year old.
But, you know, we're seeing a ton of growth, and so much interest in kind of what we're doing.
And how I am thinking about my role in this role is like, I'm really managing like the processes and kind of background systems that go into developing content for our customers.
So we, in June, launched an internal platform that leverages AI workflows to generate content, like, put out marketing content for our clients.
That's really what we're doing is, like, marketing, AI-generated growth marketing.

So thinking about this role in particular, every client is managed by a pod, which is an account strategist, a managing editor, who's kind of, like, like, small P project manager of making sure that, like, we're on track with our deliverables and things like that.
And then the content manager who's, like, running those workflows on a day-to-day basis.
Account strategists are going to be the people who are helping execute the high-level strategy that we're setting for these clients.
Taking calls, kind of laying the rails for, or laying the track for kind of where we're going with our clients long term, because one of like our growthx values is a best hire mindset.
So we think of ourselves as kind of like embedded with these clients, especially in early stages.

Some of our clients actually don't have marketing teams and really leverage us for a lot of their like marketing needs and think of us as like their real, like truly the growth engine behind leads, referrals, things like that.

So there is like some like marketing know-how component to this, but a lot of like strategic account management really just going into helping communicate, like both making sure we're on track with the strategy and then managing like.
Not directly managing the team, but managing that team's output and tracking that way as well.
Does that make sense?
Yeah, of course.
Thank you for giving me kind of just that overview entirely.
That gives me a lot more information.
But it's an exciting role.
It's new.
It's a lot of room to make impact.
In I was told that you have kind of, it's two openings.
So whoever's coming in is coming in with like a partner.
So you kind of get to like riff off of each other.
Yeah.
It sounds like such an experience.
I don't have a marketing background.
I don't either.
Okay, cool.
Because one of the things I always love about tech, MarTech is so kind of like, no, you can't with us.
But I run engagement campaigns.
And I specifically handle a book of business that does not want to talk to us because we're a compliance tool.
And when I worked at SoCal,
We are our biggest accounts were for marketing and SEO.
And that's what I was the trusted advisor on.
And I did very well, because to me, marketing is more of understanding the personas, understanding your target and what you're trying to communicate.
So for me, that was my only hesitation with even meeting up when I was reached out to on LinkedIn, because Tucker, phenomenal, had a great talk with him.
But I know in the past, when I've looked at marketing companies, and it's always been kind of in that vein.
Now, I'm a big HubSpot person, HubSpot certified.
I love using it for marketing.
I'm inbound mentality.
So there's nothing that I absolutely cannot learn.
But aside from that, everything that you all are looking for, it's what I've been doing.
I don't know how I've been stuck in startups since college, but it's where I thrive.
And it's actually what made me be very interested, even though I'm not actively.
Looking for anything.

I'll never say no to a meeting, a coffee chat, because believe me, Andi, my network is huge, because I've just always been open to a conversation, especially if it's tech or something that I'm interested in.
Love what you all are doing.
Love kind of the backstory to it.
And I do agree that there's a need for it in this market, especially in the AI scape.
And where you all are going to shine is that you're building this from a foundation with AI in mind.

So it's an AI driven company, as opposed to companies who should say, like, we're regressively integrating AI into a 30 year old company, but we're going to say a bunch of buzzwords, and you're going to get excited about it, even though, you know, this is not really functional.
Yeah, so that's exciting as well.
So yeah, I to me, thank you for the breakdown.
Thank you for the understanding of the role.
It is something that I'm very much excited about, because I get to bring in that process building experience, making a voice.
So the customer program and making sure that our product is building out for their needs, especially as they scale.
Because one thing I love about the kind of the numbers that are already publicly facing is that this is helping customer scale rapidly.
Yeah.
And that's what we want.
Yeah, totally.
Okay.
Well, tell me, I mean, maybe we start with the experience that you mentioned where you have, like, thought a lot about personas and company context and things like that.
And then we can, you know, you can maybe take a step back, too, and talk to me about, like, your customer facing experience or how you would define yourself.
Of course.
So for me, you know, I've worked in, I started out kind of in, like, hotel hospitality.
And those personas are really just one across the board.
Right.
Right.
But then when the company I was working for was acquired by Airbnb, I started working with property management company.
It's small boutique hotels and just the average everyday host on top of the guest, right?
So you think about it.
These are kind of static personas.
They're not, nothing's really changed.
Guest is looking for a great experience.
They can be one of three things.
Maybe those kind of happy-go-lucky fly by the seat of their pants.
The super OCD traveler who needs everything to be perfect.
Airbnb is not the platform to book those kinds of trips on.
This is not for you.
But, you know, we get them.
And being able to kind of navigate around them while not souring the relationship with the host or the property management company.
But when I went into my first CSM role, it's when I kind of realized different people need different approaches.
Different levels need different approaches.
I'm talking to my champion a certain way, but that C-suite CEO CISO is a much different conversation.
And the Carmen that I bring into that is going to be a different Carmen.
Now, that's kind of where I get into the persona side.
Now, going into Silk Tide.
is Mollycks� This is

I could talk to Texas Tech University, or I could talk to Dana-Farber Cancer Institute, it's a very different conversation, even though they're technically in the higher education, their needs were very different, their SEO, their accessibility design, and understanding the use case that they specifically have for my tool, and how I'm approaching them, and how I'm strategizing what success looks like for this company, as opposed to what success looks like for this company.
Now, going back into property management with Guesty, and then now at 1Password, now it's cybersecurity.
But we're a password manager who recently added identity access management, as well as device trust.
So now, when I'm talking to Apple, how am I approaching you when you have your own password manager, but you need device trust as a customer?
And how am I really selling this to you, so that I retain you, but at the end of the day, that you...
Do still feel like you're getting value, because ultimately, I don't want to lose money.
And if I have a book of business worth 3.5 million, and after a year, that hasn't moved, I failed.
I failed that year.
Because one, I didn't recognize any new growth opportunities, I didn't get them sticky enough.
And then on top of that, I'm probably not having constructive or consultative conversations, because they're not really getting any more use out of the platform than they were getting a year earlier.
Yeah, that makes a lot of sense.
So, I mean, 1Password, obviously, a much larger organization, you were working in a small organization that got acquired to Airbnb.
So, how are you thinking about, like, where you want to go next?
I know we reached out to you, but like, big company versus startup, sort of, and what you would like in your next role?
I want to build, Andy.
I want to build again.
Like,
My first sales role was startup.
I had to build all of our processes.
Some were horrible.
I integrated Salesforce in like 2010.
Not fun.
I'm not a fan of Salesforce.
I still cry about it, but everyone uses them.
So I'm just very good at Salesforce and just a little bitter about it sometimes.
But that's tech world, right?
We pick our fun ones.
I've always built processes and I left Guesty because I did not want to be siloed in property management.
I have seen as a homeowner, I've just seen what our industry has done to small cities and even small countries.
There's a lot of pushback globally.
So I left understanding that I had a lot of experience.
I'm an expert.
Nobody ever debate with me about property management, short-term rentals.
It's just my expertise because I was in it for so long.
So I left and I went to One Password because I love the tool.
I've always used it.
And I wanted to go somewhere.
into a new industry and something that I could really excel in.
I've been having a great time.
I've hit, I mean, I've hit over 100%.
This quarter, I'm at 144.
And look at where we are.
And just based on today's call that I had, probably going to blow that out because that one growth is going to be absolutely ridiculous.
And I'm very proud of that.
But that's me building relationships and continuing through and understanding their use case to be able to pivot quickly.
Because the call today was not about what it ended up being about, but we were ready to go.
So for me, it's, I want to be able to build.
I want to be able to build.
When you say, hey, you're going to build processes to make your customer love the product more and make your CSMs more efficient and happier and productive, that gets me excited.
I don't have that here.
And I'm not going to have that here because of the size.
And that's, that's just a fact.
Love one password.
I've had a great experience.
But the excitement in this role.
When I was reached out was, oh my, can I actually get back in to a smaller startup?
Silktide was under 40 people.
It was phenomenal.
I was able to build an onboarding process in my first CSM role.
They're still using it to this day.
That's the kind of stuff that gets me excited because I'll tell you, Andi, the day-to-day of being a CSM is my most natural state of being.
I am a golden retriever.
I don't know any other way of explaining what I do.
I naturally want to make my customers happy, and I can remember what they need.
So doing that part of my job is not hard.
Well, so here's the hard part of our job, and I'd love to hear an example of the other side of it.
We, Marcel, who's our CEO and has closed a lot of our deals and is still really close to the clients in a lot of instances, also a golden retriever in the best way, right?
He's closed all the I've seen videos of him.
I've seen of I've videos But like when a client says, we want to do X.
thing.
We're trying to narrow our lane of focus and get really good at one or two things before we start expanding what we're selling.

We have two work streams that I think we're honing that we'll start to be able to scale, but we want to make sure that those are tight, those processes are tight before we start selling video content or other things that we're developing.
So can you talk to me about a time when a client maybe wanted something that the product couldn't do or you didn't have and you had to push back on that?
Of course.
I think it's almost every day that we have this.
So I think, one, it's really important to set expectations.
Customers are going to forgive a lot if they've been given a heads up.
They seem to understand that, especially in our world.
Right?
There's a lot of competitors, things are changing rapidly.
And I think because of the company and kind of the product that you all have, customers don't really know what that looks like at its full scale.
So you have a lot of opportunity to educate and get them kind of on board with the growth and what this can be in the future.
I push for partnership.
And I'm big on saying that to my customers.
I want to be a partner.
I want to know that in six months, whatever roadmap is coming out, that at least one of your ideas is showing up.
And I want you to feel that we are building a product that's going to work for you.
And I'm going to be fully transparent about it, the entire process.
I'm big on having those regular sync ups, especially depending on your ARR value.
I want to see you monthly.
Based on this type of tech, I imagine you're actually meeting in much more cadences, because it is kind of like that regular content media strategy, right?
And being able to capture their feedback.
Give them those updates when you have touch points and also get internal buy-in.
So if I have a customer say to me right now where I'm currently at and they say, Carmen, I want stacked sub vaults within our main vaults.
And I need this because one of your competitors has it.
And I'm going to say, hey, that's a really good product feature request.
I'm going to go ahead and take that back to my product team.
I'll send you an update, but at the very least, I'm going to log it and we can kind of begin that discovery process.
So I'm going to go ahead, I'm going to log it, make sure that it's in product board, JIRA, wherever it is that that lives.
And I'm going to go right into whichever product manager I trust because companies are different.
When you're very small, you probably have one to three.
Here I can pick between 20 to 30, right?
And I go and I pick my product member that happens to be a senior.
And I ask them, what does it look like to get this build out?
Why haven't we?
And this is what our competitor is doing.
what
And we have several accounts that have mentioned this.
And I want to bring that ARR value.
I want you to understand if we cannot build this out within this timeframe, we are at risk of turning such and such amount, whether it's $50,000, $500,000, heaven forbid, $3 million, right?
And really bring that to product so they can say, oh, money talks.
Product tends to build a lot of times like what feels good, what sounds good, or what they think the industry is looking for.
But if we are capturing our customers' feedback, we know what they're actually asking for.
We're not just kind of like a vague data set.
So that's more how I would be approaching it.
I know having the CEO jump in is great.
I would also flag early on that we need to be able to move away from that because as we grow, because we will grow, as we grow, Marcel won't be able to do that anymore.
And it's almost like a breakup in his role and ours.
I have been in those, and Soak Tide was the same way.
I have been
He would join the calls, and he was so charming, and customers loved him, but he couldn't join every single customer sync up.
And it became a point of earning that trust and doing that handoff.

But honestly, I think it's just a matter of collecting feedback, setting expectations, always touching base on where that feedback currently sits, any improvements, and what roadblocks we are experiencing.
And even if we give them one of the five, it builds trust, and that trust builds a lot of patience.
Great.
Sorry.
Yeah, I was just taking notes, like, and I'm on a smaller screen, so I'm, like, switching windows.
Okay.
Talk to me about your organization style, how you think about structuring your work week and serving multiple clients, you know, managing a full book of business and prioritizing accordingly.
So, I handle my renewals currently.
I always like to...
One, I'm going to address anything urgent.
If my customer, no matter their ARR, mind you, I am managing strategic enterprise accounts that we're trying to get to go wall-to-wall.
I am managing extremely under-penetrated accounts that they may have 3,000 employees, but they're only using 150 licenses.
And my accounts will range from 150 ARR.
Right now, I think my lowest is 7K ARR, and they are like 1% penetrated at their company.
We are going to get 100%.
But that's the goal, right?

So when I'm looking at my book of business, understanding that they're all identified as strategic, I want to make sure that, one, there's no blockers, there's nothing that is limiting access or jeopardizing their security because breaches are happening at the most rapid rate that we've ever seen in history.
So that will be my first one.
And then if all kind of fires are put out or everything is there,
I'm now kind of identifying, is the customer request something that is an immediate need or is this like a nice to have?
I also am going to take into account ARR.

If I have five incidents of the same urgency, I am going to go after the higher ARR because that account of $150K churning because they're unhappy is a much larger impact than my account with the exact same issue that is a $6K a year.
So kind of trying to prioritize in that way.
And I also like to take, if I can get something resolved myself, then I'm probably going to get that handled a lot quicker.
When there's other moving pieces, other teammates, collaborative, cross-collaborative initiatives, then I know that I kind of have to push and I need to like get other people's buy-in.
So that may not be as highly prioritized, but I think I've become very good at managing multiple tasks almost simultaneously.
So kind of being in that call, having a live question for product, shooting it off directly while I'm on that call, and being able to say, hey, I'll get back to you, but I've already asked.
Being able to work on multiple things at one time.

If I'm working through, let's say, my spreadsheet, and I'm doing my territory planning, and I'm identifying things immediately, I'm grabbing those multiple accounts, and I'm shooting a message off the product and saying, hey, I'm throwing some time on your calendar for later this week.
Like, five accounts, this much ARR, this is going to be a problem, because our roadmap is not highlighting it.
By territory planning, I kind of get to be more proactive than I think most CSMs, because I'm already ahead of it.
So those urgent matters, they come up as one-offs.
It's not a consistent, I'm not putting out fires in my day-to-day anymore.
Well, that's great.
Fires come up.
Yeah.
I'm very much ahead of it.
If we have a breach, we
I've never had a breach.
We've had outages.
I'm sending a message out immediately to all of my customers.
Hey, there is an outage.
I will update you when it's done.
I don't need to do that.
I do it because I think that that is a partner touch.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Okay.
Talk to me about like your definition of done in terms of client deliverables and how you think about polish and sort of results and output when you're working with clients.
Do you mind repeating that question one more time?
Yeah.
So talk to me about your like definition of done in terms of client deliverables.
If you're a client something, like how do you know when a project is complete so that you can send it off?
That's a trick question.
It's never done.
The client life cycle is like a flywheel, right?
I think, I don't think a project's ever.

Done, because I think that you, you're going to, your first iteration is going to be one thing, and then you're going to collect feedback, and you're going to have a new version of it, or edits, and then you're going to get more feedback, and I think it just depends on the type of project.

If we're talking about onboarding, and I want you onboarded, and you have 100 licenses, and I want to make sure that of those 100 licenses, 75% are deployed in active weekly, and that you are using the features that are available to you.
And I am training you, right?
The smaller company, I assume you have a lot more hand-holding, and a lot more of that in-person training, or virtual training, as opposed to kind of that self-led resources.
So that is one of those projects that it does come to an end.
But it comes to an end only because you're onboarded, you're utilizing, you're hitting those daily metrics for adoption.
And now, our next project is, how can we optimize results, right?
How can we get these social...
Social media campaigns out to where we're getting this many clicks, maybe we're getting this many subscribers from it, or we're getting the click-through rate.
To be able to get those results after onboarding, it just starts a new project, which is ongoing adoption.
After adoption, now you're looking at, hey, we're like 100%.
We have results.
We have numbers.
We have data to really sell to your executives so they can see that return on investment.
What's next?
Partnership.
How can we get advocacy?
How can we get you to work with us on a case study?
How can we bring in more customers off of customer success stories and not sales?
That's what makes a sell easier than a happy customer.
I hope that answered your question.
Yeah, It's more of my philosophy on project.
Sure.
Okay, well, actually, those are all the questions I had.
So, um, do you have anything for me?
I do.
So, if you were to have a call.
I'm sorry.
like, I know I'm really energized.
It's just, I don't know.
It's like a, I'm not, I'm 37, Andi.
I'm not hiding my personality anymore.
I think I did it for a long time.
Good for you.
have very crazy, thick, curly hair.
So if you see me again, and you see me be like, is that the same person?
It is the same person.
just, today is just like a hair, hair pulled back day.
Yeah.
It's the summer.
It's hot.
It is.
It is very hot.
Like a hundred degrees.
I'm in South Carolina.

So my first question for you would be, when you think of the person for this role, I know there's two people, but if you think of the two types of people that you would like to see come into this role to really hit the ground running and just kill it, what are you, what is your ideal person?
Yeah.
So I think technical, like some technical ability is really core because we're not surfacing the product to clients right now.
We're actually leveraging the product internally.
And so you have.
Be a champion for your internal team who's using the product rather than being a champion for, like, your clients.

And sometimes that means, as you said, like, getting your hands dirty and working through things and, like, trying to figure out what's broken and what kind of feedback to bring to engineering on your own.
So, yeah, that's one piece.
And then, you know, that, like, client project management and, like, being able to juggle multiple clients and think about their priorities, hugely important.
I think we've had some people in a role that was similar to this.
We're, like, shifting our definitions of these sorts of roles because we're a startup.
We're less than a year old.
And, you know, people have had internal experience but didn't have, like, the CS, the strong CS muscle.
So, those are two things that I think are going to be really important here.
I love that.
I think, you know, I've always said that on the technical
I have been shocked at the tools that I've learned to use.
I even learned to code.
So I don't want to show my first website.
was cool, but it was like my restaurant concept.
And it was really cool.
I've been very surprised.
My husband's an engineer.
But it's an interesting one.
So for me, I always say, like, there is not a tool that I cannot learn.
You could not have told me three years ago that I would be setting up SSO and skimbridges.
And yet, here we are doing it.
So on the technical side, 100%.
For me, it's more on the skill set that I bring is definitely a good one.
It definitely sings to kind of what you seem to be looking for.
And then for me, it's do we have that training available to kind of get me ramped up?
Because once I'm in the tool, I am going to be using it.
I love the idea that we're using it internally.
And you're almost going to become a product expert before you even have a book of business.
That sounds amazing and actually very rare.
That's not an opportunity that you always get.
You normally come in, you know, hey, we need you to take over this 100 accounts and you need to hit the ground running.
And here's your, you know, your seesaw or you're learning your LMS.
So that's, that's a great one there.
So thank you.
And then how is the team currently kind of collaborating to make sure that like these customer needs are being met?
Or that like, are we collaborating, you know, weekly?
Is it a cross functional meetup?
Yeah, so we're talking to clients on a weekly basis.
Internally, we're organized, as I said, by pods.
So strategic account executive would be managing maybe two or three pods.
We were trying to do a lot async because we have like a global team, but we really need people like we've found that sometimes you just need to get on a call.
So, you know, pods have their own Slack channels.
There's a lot of async updates in Slack and things like that.
We use air.
Table for, like, organization.

And then, yeah, just, it depends on the pod, but it's managing the, like, time zones and then trying to make sure that, like, all your stuff is organized so that people can access it and read it quickly.
And just trying to unify, like, the organization's style so that no matter if you're, like, moving around pods, other people can, like, hand off and move things quickly.
Okay.
That sounds like, yeah, I've only worked for global companies.
So I think I have a call tomorrow at 10 o'clock at night in Australia.

And I always just tell my AE, you know, hey, let's, let's just try to, like, make Monday, Wednesday, like, Australia days, which is nine, from, like, 7 to 10.30 at night, we just have this block of just, like, back-to-back UBRs.
And it's worked out.
But definitely, like, I have a call in Malaysia next week, Singapore.
So supporting, like, the global clients, it's definitely been a fun one.
But, yeah, like, kind of managing it through your calendar.
Yeah.
Okay, then one question that I would have is, what is an example?
Like, I know that it's a very new company, not, like, because under 50 accounts, right?
Under 50 customers?
Yeah, I think we have, like, 35-ish right now.
Okay.
Do you have, like, a story of a customer that maybe came on board and was a skeptic?
And this might be a too-soon question.
That may have been a skeptic, but through using the tool can now be like, wow, we're a champion.
Like, I know Deep Graham has had a lot of success I've seen.
Yeah.
So I think we're, again, we're not, like, surfacing the tool for the client yet.
So it's more about the output and results.
it's more about results.
I think...
I.

I would say, yeah, we have a client called T-Row, and they're based, it's like, we talked to the CEO, and literally, we are their marketing department, and, you know, there were a couple of weeks ago where he was a churn rest, he didn't really understand marketing, was like, I'm gonna hire you guys, you guys are gonna figure it out, but then, you know, because he didn't totally understand, like, the time it takes to get results, was skeptical.

But I think, you know, we have the right tracking in place, and the right messaging for just, sometimes it takes a little bit longer to have your website, like, gain traction, get hits, start to be, like, referred, and be, get, like, good results, so that you are, like, first or second positioning in, like, search results for your set keywords.
So, you know, I think we've, like, really turned them around.
I love that.
Yeah.
I love that it's all about.
Like setting expectations, and like kind of talking to that.
I love addressing like the concerns, like just being like, hey, your concerns are so valid.
However, I want to go ahead and just kind of walk you through what this looks like if we stick it through and like put the patient.
Now, as somebody who doesn't have a marketing background, how have you been ramping up?
I know it's like two and a half months in.
So like if I was to get the job, right, and I continue.
Yeah, I think it's a little bit different.
The good news is that like the industry is changing so much right now that I don't think, you know, like people with decades of experience are also like finding themselves in new water.
